help.overview: Sensor Info shows the technical details of the currently selected sensor: who owns it, what type it is, where it sits, and the exact limits of its field of view. The icon stays disabled until you select a sensor in the Sensors menu.
help.imgAlt: The Sensor Info menu showing a sensor's location and field-of-view limits
help.imgCaption: Sensor Info for a selected radar, including its azimuth, elevation, and range limits.
help.fields:
• Latitude / Longitude: the sensor's ground location.
  • Min / Max Azimuth: the compass arc the sensor can cover.
  • Min / Max Elevation: how low and high above the horizon it can look.
  • Min / Max Range: the distance window it can track within, in km.
  • Band / Beam Width: radar frequency band and beam width; these are hidden for optical sensors and observers.
Together the azimuth, elevation, and range limits define the field-of-view volume drawn on the globe.
help.howToUse:
1. Select a sensor in the Sensors menu.
  2. Open Sensor Info from the bottom bar.
  3. Use Add Line to Sun or Add Line to Moon to draw a line from the sensor. This is handy for spotting solar exclusion windows or moonlight interference for optical sensors. Click the button again to remove the line.
help.tip1: The sun and moon line buttons need exactly one sensor selected; they will refuse to draw when a whole network is active.
help.tip2: Sensor parameters are based on publicly available data and may not be perfectly accurate. Corrections are welcome at admin@keeptrack.space.
help.tip3: To experiment with different field-of-view limits, build a copy of this sensor in the Custom Sensor menu instead, since this menu is read-only.

help.overview: Custom Sensor builds a ground sensor anywhere on Earth: a completely new site, or a modified copy of an existing one. The sensor then works everywhere a real one does, including look angles, field of view rendering, and pass calculations.
help.imgAlt: The Custom Sensor menu with location and field of view fields
help.imgCaption: Set the location and field of view, then add the sensor.
help.fields: Set the Latitude, Longitude, and Elevation Above Sea Level (Km), then pick the Type of Sensor: Observer, Optical, Phased Array Radar, or Mechanical. Checking Telescope gives a full 360 degree azimuth view with a 10 degree elevation mask and unlimited range; uncheck it to set the azimuth, elevation, and range limits yourself.
help.howToUse:
1. Open the Custom Sensor menu (Create mode), or right-click anywhere on Earth and choose Create Sensor Here or Create Observer Here to pre-fill the location.
  2. Adjust the fields, or click Use Geolocation to use your own position (HTTPS only).
  3. Click Add Custom Sensor. The new sensor appears on the globe and in the active sensor list panel.
  4. Enable Replace Sensor to swap the primary sensor instead of adding another; Clear Custom Sensors removes them all.
help.tip1: To edit a real sensor's parameters, select it in the Sensors menu first; the form pre-fills with its values so you can tweak and re-add it.
help.tip2: Multiple custom sensors stack: each addition becomes another active sensor, which is an easy way to mock up a small network.

errorMsgs.latRange: Custom Sensor Latitude must be between -90 and 90 degrees.
errorMsgs.lonRange: Custom Sensor Longitude must be between -180 and 180 degrees.
errorMsgs.altRange: Custom Sensor Altitude must be greater than or equal to 0 km.
errorMsgs.minAzRange: Custom Sensor Minimum Azimuth must be between -360 and 360 degrees.
errorMsgs.maxAzRange: Custom Sensor Maximum Azimuth must be between -360 and 360 degrees.
errorMsgs.minElRange: Custom Sensor Minimum Elevation must be between -90 and 90 degrees.
errorMsgs.maxElRange: Custom Sensor Maximum Elevation must be between -90 and 90 degrees.
errorMsgs.minRangeValue: Custom Sensor Minimum Range must be greater than or equal to 0 km.
errorMsgs.maxRangeValue: Custom Sensor Maximum Range must be greater than or equal to 0 km.

settings.showRiseSetOnly: Show Only Rise and Set Times

msgs.notVisible: Satellite is not visible for the next {hours} hours.

help.overview: Look Angles computes the pointing data a sensor needs to track the selected satellite: the time, azimuth, elevation, and range of every upcoming observation opportunity. It is the bridge between seeing a pass in 3D and actually pointing an antenna or telescope at it.
help.imgAlt: The Look Angles table of times, azimuths, elevations, and ranges
help.imgCaption: Each row is a pointing solution; click one to jump the clock to that moment.
help.reading: Each row lists the Time, elevation (El), azimuth (Az), and range (Rng) of the satellite as seen from the sensor, plus whether it is optically Visible. With Show Only Rise and Set Times enabled, the table collapses to each pass's rise, maximum elevation, and set events, labeled in the Type column.
help.howToUse:
1. Select both a sensor and a satellite; the bottom icon stays disabled until both are set.
  2. Open Look Angles from the bottom bar. The table computes automatically.
  3. Open the settings panel to toggle rise/set mode or change the Calculation Length (Days) and Interval (Seconds).
  4. Click a row to jump the simulation clock to that moment, or use the download button to export the table.
help.tip1: Rise/set mode is the fastest way to answer when you can see the satellite next; switch to the full table when you need second by second pointing angles.
help.tip2: Clicking a row preserves your playback state, so a paused clock stays paused at the new time.

msgs.notVisible: Satellite is not visible for the next {hours} hours.

help.overview: Multi-Site Look Angles answers a network question: of all my sensors, which one sees the selected satellite, and when? It computes look angles across many sites at once and interleaves them into a single chronological table.
help.imgAlt: The Multi-Site Look Angles table with sensors and pointing data
help.imgCaption: Each row pairs a pass moment with the sensor that sees it.
help.reading: Rows are sorted chronologically across every enabled site, each listing the Time, elevation (El), azimuth (Az), range (Rng), the Sensor involved, and optical visibility. The sensor toggle panel chooses which sites are computed: green sensors are included and red sensors are excluded.
help.howToUse:
1. Select a satellite; the bottom icon stays disabled until one is selected. No sensor selection is needed.
  2. Open Multi-Site Looks from the bottom bar. The default missile warning network is computed automatically.
  3. Toggle sensors on or off in the sensor list panel to change the network.
  4. Click a row to select that sensor and jump the clock to that moment, or export the table with the download button.
help.tip1: Use it to plan handoffs: where one sensor's coverage ends, the next row shows which site picks the satellite up.
help.tip2: Optical sites show visibility only when lighting conditions allow an actual observation; radars always show N/A.
